1:
The chapter opens on a summary of attrition, and the witnesses read the 'long war' less as a sequence of pitched battles than as a protracted state of rival claims. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott insists there was 'not actual fighting of pitched battles' but a standing hostility in which Ish-bosheth and David each claimed the allegiance of the whole nation, Ish-bosheth too weak to press real war and David 'content to abide the fulfilment of the promises of the Lord in His own good time.' He traces David's steady gain to a growing appreciation of his character and prowess and a fuller sense that he was the divinely appointed sovereign, citing the important accession from Manasseh and the men who came to David 'day by day' (1 Chron. 12:19–22), the necessary result of which was that 'the house of Saul waxed weaker and weaker.'

The Reformed John Gill and the Wesleyan John Wesley fix the chronology. The battle at Gibeon had gone heavily for David, yet it did not end the war, which ran five years more; not until Ish-bosheth's death, when David had reigned above seven years in Hebron, was he at last made king over all Israel. Wesley reckons Ish-bosheth crowned soon after Saul's fall, the northern tribes holding out the full seven years before they would submit.

The Puritan Matthew Henry supplies the theological weight. He wonders that a cause with right, and therefore God, on its side should be a long war at all, and answers that God, for wise and holy ends, prolonged the conflict to try the faith and patience of David and make his settlement the more welcome. Then he turns the history into a mirror of the believer: the contest between grace and corruption in a heart 'sanctified but in part' is just such a long war, the flesh lusting against the spirit and the spirit against the flesh (Gal. 5:17); but as sanctification advances, corruption, like the house of Saul, grows weaker and weaker, while grace, like the house of David, grows stronger and stronger, till judgment be brought forth unto victory. Adam Clarke, sketching the chapter, reads the same 'frequent battles and skirmishes' running to the fifth year and Ish-bosheth's murder. The witnesses do not dispute; together they hold that the slow triumph of David is both a fact of history and a pledge that God's promises, though they tarry, prevail.
2:
With the list of sons the narrative offers, in the witnesses' reading, documentary evidence of the rising house. Keil and Delitzsch stress that the register 'does not break the thread,' as some had complained, but is 'very appropriately introduced here, as a practical proof of the strengthening of the house of David.' They note the historian's habitual custom of opening the reign of a king with notices of his family, comparing the similar lists elsewhere (2 Sam. 5:13; 1 Kings 3:1) and the parallel roll of David's sons (1 Chron. 3:1–4). The first two sons, they observe, were born of the two wives David had brought with him to Hebron (1 Sam. 25:42–43), and they pause over a copyist's slip in the Hebrew of the verb 'were born.'

The firstborn is Amnon, of Ahinoam the Jezreelitess — a name the reader will meet again in sorrow, as Keil hints by noting the softened form 'Aminon' used later (2 Sam. 13:20). The Reformed John Gill adds the domestic detail that grounds the whole notice: David 'was married before he came' to Hebron and 'had wives in his state of exile, but had no children by them there, at least no sons.' The very barrenness of the wilderness years throws the fruitfulness of Hebron into relief.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the six sons of the Hebron years, reads them as reinforcement: every child whose welfare was bound up in the common safety was 'a fresh security given to the commonwealth,' for the man whose quiver is filled with such arrows 'shall speak with his enemy in the gate' (Ps. 127:5). Yet he will not flatter David, marking already the fault that these came of several wives, contrary to the law (Deut. 17:17), and a bad example to his successors.

There is no debate here, and the witnesses are few, but their shared point is quietly weighty. The birth of sons is not mere genealogy; it is, in this season of civil war, a sign of settled favour and continuance. Where the sons of Saul were being cut off, the sons of David were being given, and the historian's plain list becomes, under the commentators' eye, one more line in the argument that the kingdom was passing, by God's hand, from the fading house to the growing one.
3:
The witnesses linger over two of these sons — the second, with his double name, and the third, with his foreign mother. On Chileab, the Reformed John Gill explains the puzzle that the same son is called Daniel in the Chronicler's list (1 Chron. 3:1). Daniel, 'God hath judged me,' was the first name, given because the Lord had judged David's cause against Nabal and pleaded him out of his reproach (1 Sam. 25:39); Chileab, 'like unto his father' or 'all father,' Gill takes of a son who bore his father's features exactly — though he waves off as 'fabulous' the rabbinic story that gave rise to the name.

Absalom's mother draws the sharpest comment. She is Maacah, daughter of Talmai king of Geshur, and the Wesleyan John Wesley reads the marriage as statecraft: Geshur was 'a part of Syria, northward from the land of Israel,' and David wed its princess, 'as it may seem, in policy, that he might have a powerful friend and ally' against Ish-bosheth's party in the north while he himself pressed the southern parts. But Wesley will not let the policy pass unjudged — 'he paid dear for making piety give place to policy, as the history of Absalom sheweth.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep the register in order, giving each son and mother in turn, before they turn at the sixth son to the decline of the house of Saul and to Abner, who 'made himself strong for the house of Saul' even as he privately appropriated one of Saul's concubines. For them the list is above all evidence, the roll of a house on the rise.

Yet the witnesses converge on a moral the text only implies. The genealogy is not neutral. Each name that the commentators pause upon carries a shadow: Chileab reminds them of the reproach of Nabal answered by God, and Absalom of a marriage of convenience that would one day recoil upon the king in rebellion and grief. The record of the strengthening house already holds, for these readers, the seeds of its later sorrows, so that the strength of Hebron and the tragedy of Jerusalem are written in the same short list.
4:
The register continues with the fourth and fifth sons, and the witnesses' interest fixes almost entirely on the fourth. Adonijah the son of Haggith is, for the Reformed John Gill, no obscure name but a note of foreboding: this is 'the same that usurped the throne before his father's death, to anticipate Solomon, and died by his order' (1 Kings 1:5; 1 Kings 2:25). What the historian records as a bare birth in Hebron, Gill reads with the whole later story in view — the child now numbered among David's strength will become, like Absalom before him, a grief and a danger to his house.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep the list in its place, giving each son and mother in turn — Adonijah of Haggith, the fifth Shephatiah of Abital — as part of the family notice that, in their reading, documents the rising fortunes of David. For them the very placement of the register is the argument: it stands where a historian would naturally record the establishment of a reign, and its steady enumeration of sons is a quiet witness that David's house is being built up while his rival's decays. The mothers are named but otherwise unknown, and the commentators do not speculate beyond what the text supplies.

With so spare a verse the witnesses do not divide; they only underline a pattern. Three of these six sons — Amnon, Absalom, and Adonijah — would come to infamy, and the sober reader is meant to feel the irony. The multiplication of wives and sons that looked like security carried its own future troubles. As Matthew Henry observes of the whole list, we have reason 'to rejoice with trembling in the building up of our families,' for numerous offspring is no guarantee of honour or comfort, and here the very names that swell David's house are the names that will one day wound it. Yet the point is not fatalism but watchfulness: the commentators read the genealogy forward, letting each name preach its own later chapter, so that the quiet list of Hebron becomes a warning against measuring a man's true strength by the mere size of his household.
5:
The sixth and last son, Ithream, is born of Eglah, and the witnesses fasten on the curious tag attached to her name alone: 'David's wife.' Why single her out when all six were wives? The Reformed John Gill canvasses the options. Perhaps she was 'a person of no note, and had nothing else to distinguish her' — though he grants the same could be said of others in the list. Perhaps she was his beloved wife, his 'heifer,' as her name signifies. The rabbis, he reports, took her for Michal, David's first wife, whom he greatly loved; but Gill rejects this on plain grounds, since Michal had not yet been returned to David at Hebron, and as his first wife she would hardly be reckoned last. He dismisses too the further Jewish fancy that she was Saul's widow.

The Wesleyan John Wesley weighs the same possibilities and inclines, more gently than Gill, toward the Michal reading: Eglah is named with the distinguishing phrase either because she was 'of obscure parentage,' known only by her relation to David, or because she was 'his first and most proper wife, best known by her other name of Michal.' Michal, he notes, had no child after she scoffed at David's dancing before the ark (2 Sam. 6:23), 'yet might have one before that time'; and she might be named last precisely 'because she was given away from David, and married to another man.'

Beneath the small dispute over Eglah's identity the two witnesses agree on the larger lesson of the whole list. Wesley draws it out: six sons in seven years, yet 'some have had as numerous an offspring, and with much more honour and comfort, by one wife.' Not one of the six became famous, and three — Amnon, Absalom, Adonijah — became infamous. Gill for his part refuses even to relate the Jewish tales told of Eglah, dismissing them as unworthy of report, so that both witnesses prefer to rest in what the verse plainly says. The register that opened as a token of David's growing strength closes, in the commentators' hands, as a quiet caution against the polygamy that multiplied the king's sorrows even as it multiplied his sons.
6:
The verse turns from David's household to the man who propped up his rival, and the witnesses divide gently over what Abner's growing strength really meant. All agree on the fact stated: 'Abner made himself strong for the house of Saul.' The grammatical-historical commentators explain both the phrase and its narrative purpose. Keil and Delitzsch parse the idiom — to 'prove one's self strong for' a person is to render him powerful help — and read the whole notice as prelude to the rupture: Abner adhered firmly to Saul's house, yet had 'certainly perceived the utter incapacity of Ishbosheth' from very early, and had probably made him king only to rule through him. Charles Ellicott adds that the fortunes of that house 'depended entirely upon Abner,' so that the fact is set down here precisely to explain the story that follows.

The Wesleyan John Wesley takes the strengthening at face value as loyal exertion — Abner 'used all his endeavours to support Saul's house' — and notes that the historian records it to show the depth of Abner's resentment when he is soon reproached. His fellow Wesleyan Adam Clarke, however, reads a darker motive: the self-strengthening, together with going in to the late king's concubine, were 'most evident proofs that he wished to seize upon the government,' for to take a royal concubine was to lay claim to the throne (compare 1 Kings 2:21–22).

The Reformed John Gill stands between, simply marking the time-frame: the war lasted 'as long as that continued,' until the following quarrel between Ish-bosheth and Abner broke it open. Keil adds the grammatical detail that verses 6 and 7 form a single, expanded sentence, the whole clause about Abner's strength and Saul's concubine hanging in suspense until Ish-bosheth's charge in the next verse gives it point — the historian withholding the outburst to heighten it. The real difference among the witnesses is over Abner's heart — whether his power was single-minded service (Wesley, Ellicott) or already an ambition reaching toward the crown (Clarke) — but they are one in seeing that the house of Saul now rested on a single overburdened man, and that when he should fail it, it would fall of course.
